coredata.dc:title: Extraction and selective precipitation of metal ions from LiCoO2 cathodes using citric acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Cerrillo-Gonzalez, Maria del Mar
Paz-Garcia, Juan Manuel
Muñoz-Espinosa, Maria
Rodriguez-Maroto, Jose Miguel
Villen-Guzman, Maria
coredata.dc:description: The most common methods for recycling Lithium-Ion Batteries use strong mineral acids and hydrogen peroxide. Alternative acids and reducing agents are currently being studied to reduce the environmental impact of these batteries recycling processes. This research provides a practical pathway for recycling batteries using citric acid as a sustainable lixiviant. The combination of citric acid and copper as extractant and reductant agents, respectively, to selectively recover lithium and cobalt from LiCoO2 has been studied. Leaching with 2 M citric acid and 70 °C in the presence of copper resulted in a recovery of nearly 100 % Li and around 81 % Co, contained in the liquid phase and the solid precipitate formed during the experiments. Cobalt solubility experiments and solid characterization were performed to understand further the formation of cobalt-citrate solid species detected. Experimental and theoretical results suggest that the precipitate could be identified as CoHCitrate, associated with the selective recovery of cobalt from LiCoO2. Results obtained in this work indicate that citric acid could be used not only as an extractant agent but also as a precursor to precipitate metals from LIBs, improving the efficiency and selectivity of recycling processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Recycling
Leaching
Citric acid
Copper
Citrate-cobalt complexes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of the citric acid concentration
Cobalt solubility in citric acid solution

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has received funding from the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under the Marie Skłodowska-Curie grant agreement No. 778045. Authors acknowledge the funding from the University of Málaga (B1-2021_35). Authors also acknowledge the support received by the SCAI-UMA (Malaga, Sapin) to perform the FTIR and UV–Vis measurements. Cerrillo-Gonzalez acknowledges the FPU2018/04295 grant obtained from the Spanish Ministry of Universities. Partial funding for open access charge: Universidad de Málaga / CBUA.

This work has received funding from the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under the Marie Skłodowska-Curie grant agreement No. 778045 . Authors acknowledge the funding from the University of Málaga ( B1-2021_35 ). Authors also acknowledge the support received by the SCAI-UMA (Malaga, Sapin) to perform the FTIR and UV–Vis measurements. Cerrillo-Gonzalez acknowledges the FPU2018/04295 grant obtained from the Spanish Ministry of Universities . Partial funding for open access charge: Universidad de Málaga / CBUA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the experimental procedures in this study.
Effect of temperature on leaching of (a) lithium and (b) cobalt; (c) pH evolution (Conditions: 0.5 M citric acid, pulp density of 50 g/L).
Effect of citric acid concentration on leaching of lithium and cobalt at (a) 2 h and (b) 24 h; (c) pH evolution (Conditions: 70 °C, 50 g/L).
Effect of Cu addition on the leaching of lithium and cobalt (a) without Cu (A8); (b) 1 mol Cu/1 mol LiCoO2 (A9). (Conditions: 2 M citric acid, 70 °C, pulp density of 50 g/L).
Final distribution of lithium, cobalt and copper referred to the metal content in the initial solid.
(a) FTIR-ATR and (b) UV–Vis spectrum of precipitate samples.
Effect of pH on the solubility of cobalt in a saturate citric acid solution.
Experimental conditions used in metal extraction experiments.

Equilibrium reactions for citric acid and cobalt-citrate complexes (
K
e q
for concentrations in M).
Composition of solid precipitate and different cobalt citrate complex salts.
Solubility product of citrate-cobalt salts and reaction quotient of experimental solubility tests.

head.ce:title: Extraction and selective precipitation of metal ions from LiCoO2 cathodes using citric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Malaga, Malaga, Spain
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Malaga, Malaga, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The most common methods for recycling Lithium-Ion Batteries use strong mineral acids and hydrogen peroxide. Alternative acids and reducing agents are currently being studied to reduce the environmental impact of these batteries recycling processes. This research provides a practical pathway for recycling batteries using citric acid as a sustainable lixiviant. The combination of citric acid and copper as extractant and reductant agents, respectively, to selectively recover lithium and cobalt from LiCoO2 has been studied. Leaching with 2 M citric acid and 70 °C in the presence of copper resulted in a recovery of nearly 100 % Li and around 81 % Co, contained in the liquid phase and the solid precipitate formed during the experiments. Cobalt solubility experiments and solid characterization were performed to understand further the formation of cobalt-citrate solid species detected. Experimental and theoretical results suggest that the precipitate could be identified as CoHCitrate, associated with the selective recovery of cobalt from LiCoO2. Results obtained in this work indicate that citric acid could be used not only as an extractant agent but also as a precursor to precipitate metals from LIBs, improving the efficiency and selectivity of recycling processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The transition to a decarbonised and clean energy system is related to an increase of the demand of batteries for energy storage, especially lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Their technical features have made them the best option for electric transportation and stationary energy storage. However, the widespread consumption of LIBs is also related to increased demand for materials and metals. For example, the cobalt and lithium annual demand for electric vehicle batteries is expected to increase from 140 kt and 77 kt in 2020 to 370 kt and 360 kt in 2030, respectively [1]. Besides their economic importance, the fact that these metals are extracted only in a few countries limits the development of the battery industry due to their dependence on imported supplies [2]. Hence, the scarcity of raw metals, their high prices, and the supply risk due to geopolitical concerns emphasise the importance of recovering them from LIBs batteries waste, contributing to the circular economy principles [3].

Current LIB's recycling processes were developed based on metallurgy techniques [4]. These processes typically submit the batteries to mechanical and thermal pre-treatment to facilitate the dissolution step via acid leaching. Once metals are in solution, the valuable components are selectively separated by precipitation, solvent extraction, or electrochemical methods [5,6]. Among the steps listed above, the leaching is considered the limiting factor of the recycling process. Leaching electrode active material from battery waste requires the use of acid solutions together with a reductant agent to facilitate the extraction of the target metal ions. For example, leaching of LiCoO2 particles with stoichiometric sulfuric acid and hydrogen peroxide results in:
(1)
LiCo
O 2
s
+
3 2
H 2
SO 4
+
1 2
H 2
O 2
⇄
Li +
+
Co
2 +
+ 2
H 2
O +
1 2
O 2
g
+
3 2
SO 4
2 −
where Co(III) has been reduced to the soluble form Co(II).

Different leaching agents have been widely investigated, such as H2SO4, HCl and HNO3 [7] with metal recoveries near 100 % in the presence of a reductant agent. However, these strong acids release toxic gases like SO3, Cl2 and NOX during the leaching process that, in addition to the waste acid solution generated, entails a threat for the environment and industrial safety [8,9].

Hydrogen peroxide, H2O2, is the most common and predominant reductant in the LIBs recycling industry [10], especially for the recovery of cobalt ions. H2O2, is known as a strong oxidizing agent (H2O2/H2O;
E 0
=
1.776 V). However, cobalt in the LiCoO2 particles are in the stable form of Co (III) and require reduction to Co (II) for its dissolution (Co3+/Co2+;
E 0
=
1.93 V). Due to the high reduction potential of Co (III), H2O2 acts as reducing agent against it, normally through oxygen evolution (O2/H2O2,
E 0
=
0.695 V).

As shown in Eq (1), the use of H2O2 as a reducing agent for cobalt ions has the advantage of producing no additional ions in the system. However, H2O2 has certain drawbacks. The current anthraquinone process to produce H2O2 is costly and not environmentally sustainable [11]. Moreover, the presence of oxidizable species in the battery waste, such as metallic Cu, Al and Fe, would decrease the selectivity of H2O2, which means an increase in the chemical consumption, environmental impact and cost related to H2O2 [12]. Furthermore, the reactivity between H2O2 and the possible organic acids that could substitute the strong mineral acids is typically high. All these difficulties motivate the search of alternatives leaching and reductant agents to develop more benign processes and reduce the environmental impacts of battery recycling industry [13].

One alternative to the use of mineral acid for LIB leaching is the use of organic acids due to their natural non-toxic degradation [8,14,15]. The most investigated organic acids for leaching metals from LIBs are succinic [16], lactic [17], citric [18], malic [19], and ascorbic acid [20], being citric acid the cheapest and most readily available acid with excellent leaching performance [21]. Citric acid, C6H8O7, is a common weak acid with three carboxyl groups per molecule. Moreover, it is a good chelating agent with metal ions, making them more soluble [22].

After leaching LiCoO2 with citric acid, Li+ and Co2+ are in the solution together with different citric acid species, which can react to form complexes. Yu et al. [23] detected the formation of cobalt-citrate complexes during the leaching process of LiCoO2, after drying the leaching solution and characterizing the final solid by Fourier Transform Infrared and UV spectroscopy. They concluded that the final solid was a mixture of citrates and citric acid, in coordination with the metals. Therefore, the chelation of metals with citric acid not only plays an important role in improving the leaching efficiency but also in producing precursors to synthetizes new cathodes materials for LIBs [24].

On the other hand, the use of reductant agent to the leaching system is still required when using organic acids. As mentioned before, H2O2 is the most common used reducing agent, but it may react with the citrates instead of with the target ion. Other substances investigated as possible reductants are NaHSO3 [25], methanol [26], glucose [27], ascorbic acid [18,28] as well as some biomass such as tea waste [21]. Although all these compounds could act as reductant, the addition of extra compounds to the system could involve extra stages to removal them after leaching process and an increase in the chemical consumption.

One alternative to avoid the addition of external reductants for LIBs leaching is the utilization of metallic fragments already existing in the battery waste. In a LIB, the anode and the cathode are cast over a layer of Cu and Al foil, respectively, which act as current collectors. The active materials (e.g., LiCoO2 and graphite), the conductive additive (e.g., carbon black), and the current collectors (Al and Cu foil), are difficult to separate during the pre-treatment before the leaching step in the recycling processes. Therefore, the grinded material is normally mixed with small flakes of Al and Cu foil. The separation of Al and Cu from the particles is costly and difficult, and they are found as contaminants in leaching process performed with H2O2.

Investigations by Peng et al. [29,30] proved that impurities of copper and aluminium can effectively improve the acid dissolution of target active materials. Joulie et al. [31] studied the reductive capability of these metals separately to dissolve LiNi0.3Mn0.3Co0.3O2 (NMC) in an acidic media. The results reported an improvement in the leaching efficiencies of Co, Ni and Mn from 40 % to approximately 90 % with the addition of metallic Cu to the leaching system. Similar results were obtained by Meng et al. [18] who studied the leaching of an industrial spent LIBs scraps using citric acid. The solid was a mixture of anode, cathode (composed of NMC), electrolyte and separator. They observed that the presence of Cu and Al in the initial solid promotes the leaching of metals, and about 91 % Li, 90 % Co, 94 % Ni and 89 % Mn was extracted under the optimum leaching conditions (0.5 M citric acid, 80 g/L solid to liquid ratio, 90 °C and 80 min).

In this work, the use of citric acid as an extracting agent to the selective recovery of cobalt from LIBs cathodes was evaluated. The effect of temperature and the concentration of the citric acid were studied to optimize the leaching conditions. In addition to this, the use of copper as a reductant agent in combination to the citric acid was also studied. The formation of citrate cobalt complexes salt was evaluated through experimental solubility tests and model results based on the equilibrium reactions of citrate and cobalt-citrate species.

In recycling processes with strong mineral acids and H2O2, the metal ions in the leachate are precipitated with carbonates through the controlled addition of, for example, Li2CO3 and LiOH. After that, the carbonates, such as CoCO3, are calcinated (500-900 °C) to synthesize pure LiCoO2 powder for the fabrication of new batteries. The formation of cobalt citrate precipitates directly in the leaching step could facilitate the precipitation process, offer possibilities for selective separation and decrease the cost in reactants. Cobalt citrate precipitates could be treated thermically to produce new active material, in a similar way than the carbonates.

The main novelty within this work is, therefore, the extraction of cobalt using citric acid and its precipitation as citrates in a single step, without adding carbonates to the system. The results also show that copper, already existing in the battery waste, can be used as reducing agent in the process. The promising strategy proposed here would enable the decrease of reactive consumption in the stages after the leaching.

Extraction experiments were carried out to evaluate the effect of three different parameters (temperature, citric acid, and copper concentration) on cobalt and lithium extraction using a one factor at a time methodology (OF@T). This method consists in changing the value of one parameter while other parameters are fixed, and the optimum value obtained is used to study the next parameters. All experiments are listed in Table 1 .

All leaching experiments were carried out in 500 mL Erlenmeyer flasks, installed inside a shaker bath to keep the suspension mixed, and to control the temperature. Erlenmeyer flasks were sealed to avoid mass loss during the reaction. Once the temperature reached the target value, 5 g of LiCoO2 (97 % Alsa easer, Germany) was added to 100 mL of citric acid (1-hydrate PanReac, Spain) solution (i.e, solid-liquid ratio of 50 g L-1). In the experiment A9, an amount of powder copper (99 % Sigma Aldrich) was also added in the leaching suspension together with the LiCoO2. The experiments were carried out for 24h, collecting samples of 1 mL at different times (5,10,20,30,45,60,120,1440 min) to obtain a transient profile for the study of the leaching rate. For some experimental conditions, the formation of a precipitate was detected. At the end of the experiments, the suspension was filtered with a vacuum filtration assembly (Boeco R-300). The leached solid waste was dried at 70 °C for 24h prior further characterization. Toward a better understanding of the composition of the formed solid, solubility tests were performed as explained in the next section. Regarding the liquid phase, the filtrate was collected to quantify the final volume of the solution. Each experiment was performed by triplicate to ensure the reproducibility. An overview of the experimental procedure followed in this research is presented in Fig. 1 .

The solubility of Co (II) in citric acid solutions at different pH was measured using the shake-flask method [32]. First, a solution of 2 M of citric acid was prepared (pH = 1), using 50 mL of solution for each pH studied. With the aim of avoiding the addition of any metals that could interact with the citrate species, Co(OH)2 (95 % Sigma-Aldrich) was used for both, adjust the pH of the solution and add the metallic ions. Once the target pH of each solution was reached (2, 2.1, 3.3 and 6.4), the solutions were stirred, and after 4 h, a precipitate solid appeared in the samples with pH 2.1, 3.3 and 6.4. Then, an amount of each precipitate solid in excess was added to 50 ml of water to ensure the saturation of the medium. The new suspensions were stirred for 4 h, and after that, the samples were filtered and analysed. All the experiments were carried out at a 25 °C.

In order to further study the effect of pH on cobalt solubility in citric acid, the distribution of citrate and cobalt-citrate species was obtained by thermodynamic calculation. Table 2 collects the chemical equilibrium reactions used for the simulation, which were carried out assuming 25 °C and 1atm. In this model, the equations were described based on the equilibrium constants. The pH and total cobalt concentration were set from the results obtained in the solubility experiments, while the initial concentration of each citrate and cobalt-citrate complex species was initially set as unknown. The activity coefficient was assumed to be 1.

Samples obtained during the experiments were filtered after being withdrawn using 0.6 microm glass-fiber (Macherey-Nagel (MN) GF-3, Düren, Germany) for metal quantitative analysis via atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AAS, Agilent 55AA, Australia). The percentage of metal extracted was determined according to Eq. (10):
(10)
x
t , i
=
C
t , i
V r
m
0 ,
i
x 100
where x
t,i (%w/w) is the percentage of metal extracted, C
t,i (mg L−1) is the dissolved metal concentration (analysed by AAS), V
r (L) is the solution volume and m
0,i (mg) is the initial amount of metal in solid sample. The total content of metal in the solid samples was determine by microwave-assisted acid digestion following the guidelines of the EPA method 3051A: a representative sample of up to 100 mg was dissolved in 9 mL concentrated nitric acid and 3 mL concentrated hydrochloric acid for 10 min using microwave heating.

All results were obtained taking account the change of the volume due to the sampling and evaporation. The pH of samples was measured by a pHmeter (HACH sensION pH 3, Spain), calibrated for each temperature. With the aim of quantifying the amount of carbon and hydrogen contained in the solid, the solid precipitated during leaching experiments A8 and A9 (Table 1) were analysed by the CHN technique (Leco TruSpec Micro CHNSO, Germany). Moreover, the solid samples obtained during leaching test A8 and A9 and solubility tests were characterized by FTIR-ATR (6800FV Jasco, Japan) and UV–Vis analyser (Cary7000 Agilent, Australia).

The effect of the temperature on the dissolution of LiCoO2 particles using citric acid was studied fixing acid concentration and solid/liquid ratio at 0.5 M and 50 gL-1, respectively, and varying the temperature at 30, 50, 70 and 90 °C (experiments A1, A2, A3 and A4, respectively). Fig. 2 summarizes the leaching results for lithium and cobalt. It can be observed that the percentage of metal extracted is improved by increasing the temperature. At temperatures lower than 50 °C, the amount of each metal dissolved is below 10 % for 2 h of experiment. The leaching efficiency for both metals is significantly enhanced when the temperature is increased from 50 °C to 90 °C, obtaining 40.4 % Li and 24.7 % Co after 2 h. One explanation of this trend could be that the citric acid dissociation process is favoured by the temperature, so there would be more H+ in the solution [33]. However, the pH of the initial solutions for all temperatures is approximately 1.5 (Fig. 2c). These results are in line with the species distribution of citric acid obtained at different temperatures using the free software PHREEQC [34], shown in Fig. S1. From simulation results obtained for 30 and 90 °C, it could be concluded that the species distribution is not dependent on the temperature. As can be observed, the dominant species at pH below 1.5 is H3Citrate for both temperatures. Therefore, the improvement observed with the temperature may be associated with the endothermicity of metal leaching processes [35] which could explain the low percentage of metals extracted at 30 °C. Although the same concentration of protons is available in the medium (the initial pH value was the same for all temperatures studied), the activation energy is not enough to start the reaction under low temperature conditions.

In terms of leaching efficiency, the highest percentage is obtained at 90 °C. However, for further experiments the reaction temperature was fixed at 70 °C due to the energy consumption and the evaporation losses of solution are lower.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the citric acid concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of citric acid concentration on the leaching of lithium and cobalt is presented in Fig. 3 for an experimental temperature of 70 °C and a S/L ratio of 50 g/L. The increase of the acid concentration from 0.25 to 2 M results in an improvement of the Li leaching efficiency from 19.1 % to 45.4 % after 2 h of experiments as shown in Fig. 3a. On the other hand, the percentage of cobalt extracted is almost constant (around 20 %) when the acid concentration is above 0.5 M. As can be concluded from experimental results, the extraction of Li and Co does not take place in a 1:1 proportion which has been reported in previous works [36,37]. This phenomenon is associated with the formation of a Co3O4 crust around the LiCoO2 particles which limits the diffusion of protons [6,38].

From experimental results obtained after 24 h (Fig. 3b), it is observed that the extraction of both metals, lithium and cobalt, is enhanced from 28.8 % to 64.2% and 25.7 % to 52.0%, respectively, when the initial acid concentration is increased from 0.25 to 1.5 M. These results could be related to the slow dissolution of the formed crust around the LiCoO2 particles in the presence of protons. The formation of metal complex with the citrate (Eq(5)-(7)) could entail a shift of the dissociation equilibrium (Eq. (2)-(4)) for citric acid. This phenomenon leads to higher availability of protons in the aqueous solution as can be concluded from Fig. 3c, in which the final pH for experiments carried out at higher concentrations (from 1 M to 2 M) were around 2.5, the buffer region of H3Citrate.

However, the percentage of cobalt in aqueous solution decreases from 52.0 % to 12.0 % when the acid concentration is increased from 1.5 to 2 M while the lithium percentage increased from 64.2 % to 70.4 %. These results are associated with the formation of a precipitate after 24 h. With the aim of quantifying the metal contained in the solid, once the experiments were finished, the solid phase was separated and dried. The solid phase, consisting of the precipitated formed and LiCoO2 particles undissolved, was mixed with water to quantify the fraction of metals contained in the precipitate. From the analysis of the liquid phase after solubilizing precipitate formed in water, it is confirmed the presence of a 56.5 % of Co in the precipitate in relation to the initial cobalt concentration in LiCoO2. Therefore, the total solubilized cobalt from LiCoO2 was 68.6 % (discontinuous dot in Fig. 3b), considering the cobalt in the final leaching solution and in the precipitate. More details about the analysis of the solid formed during leaching experiments are presented in section “3.1.4 Precipitate characterization”.

The effect of copper as a reductant agent to leach metals from LiCoO2 was studied, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . During the leaching process, the temperature was fixed at 70 °C, the S/L ratio was 50 g/L and the initial citric acid concentration was 2 M. Cu was added in a molar ratio 1:1 respect to the LiCoO2 in the system.

From experimental results, it can be observed that the presence of copper can effectively enhance the percentage of metals extracted in the leaching process. During the first 6 h, the percentage of lithium and cobalt increases, respectively, from 59.6 % and 38.1 % (without Cu) to 73.3 % and 67.0 % (with Cu). In addition, it should be noted that the extraction of Li and Co kept a 1:1 proportion in the presence of copper, which could be due to the spontaneous redox reaction between cobalt and copper, taking into account the values of the metal reduction potentials (eq.(11)–(14)):
(11)
C
o
3 +
+
e −
→ C
o
2 +
+ 1.92
v s
S H E
(12)
C
u +
+
e −
→ C
u
( s )
+ 0.52
v s
S H E
(13)
C
u
2 +
+
2 e
−
→ C
u
( s )
+ 0.34
v s
S H E
(14)
C
u
2 +
+
e −
→ C
u +
+ 0.16
v s
S H E

These results are associated with the role of copper as reductant agent in front of cobalt, where each atom of copper can donate two electrons to two atoms of cobalt [39,40]. It could explain why the percentage of copper dissolved was 34.3 % while the cobalt extracted is almost double after 6 h of experiments.

In absence of copper, a precipitate was observed after 24 h, which resulted in a decrease of cobalt concentration in solution while lithium concentration stayed constant. A similar phenomenon is observed in presence of copper, with the difference that the precipitate is detected after only 8 h of the experiments instead of at 24 h. Therefore, from the experimental results, it can be concluded that the presence of copper enhances the rate extraction of cobalt by 1.5 times. In turn, a higher concentration of cobalt in solution promotes the precipitation of the metal ions. For both cases, absence/presence of Cu, the final percentage of metals in solution after 24h was similar: 71.3 % of Li and 11.3 % of Co for A8; 79.0 % of Li and 10.1 % of Co for A9. After analysing the precipitates of test A8 and A9, it was found that the precipitate appeared in absence of Cu (experiment A8) contains the 53 % and 10.0 % of Co and Li, respectively, from the initial LiCoO2 particles. The precipitate from experiment carried out adding Cu (A9) contains 71.1 % and 18.7 % of Co and Li, respectively, from the initial LiCoO2 particles. Moreover, precipitate from test A9 contains impurities of Cu, related with the formation of copper-citrate complex and the undissolved copper. In both cases, the metals contained in the precipitate were firstly dissolved from the LiCoO2 particles. Once the medium was saturated with cobaltous ions, and the pH reached the value of 2.36, the formation of the precipitate was favoured. Therefore, the total amount of Li and Co extracted from LiCoO2 (including the metal in aqueous solution and in the precipitate) is 81.3 % and 64.5 % in A8 while the percentage of Li and Co is 97.8 % and 81.3 % in A9, respectively. Fig. 5 shows the final distribution of each metal in the test A8 and A9. From results, it could be concluded that the presence of Cu as reductant entails an improvement in the dissolution of cobalt and lithium. These results suggest that the use of current collectors can be explored as efficient and sustainable reductant agents in LIBs leaching without the need for additional reactive.

To analyse the precipitate formed during the leaching experiments A8 and A9, a known mass of the final solid was dissolved in water. It was obtained that a fraction of the final solid was easily dissolved in water while a black solid remained as solid phase, which is associated with LiCoO2 and Cu (in the case of test A9) undissolved during the leaching process. The aqueous solution was analysed by atomic absorption to quantify the metal content of the precipitate. It was obtained that the composition of the precipitate obtained for the experiment A8 is 18 %w/w of Co and 0.3%w/w of Li (dry basis), and the composition of the precipitate obtained for the experiment A9 is 12.69 % of Co, 6.24%w/w of Cu and 0.3 % Li (dry basis).

The metal contained in the solid precipitate was firstly leached from LiCoO2 particles and once in solution, cobalt and copper would form complexes with citrate species which could entail the precipitation of these species from saturated solutions. The solid samples were submitted to elemental CHN analysis to determine carbon and hydrogen content in the precipitate solid. The CHN composition of precipitate solids obtained in leaching experiments in the absence (A8) and the presence of Cu (A9) is reported in Table 3 . Comparing the results with the composition of two cobalt-citrate salts, CoHCitrate and Co3Citrate2, it may be confirmed the solid species formed during leaching experiments could be a cobalt-citrate salt.

Once confirmed the presence of carbon and hydrogen in the precipitate, and with the aim of investigate the possibility of formation of metal-citrate complexes, the precipitate was characterized by FT-IR and UV–Visible techniques. The FT-IR spectrum of both samples of precipitate, A8 and A9, is shown in Fig. 6 a. Both spectrums are similar, being both dominated by the signal appeared at 1391 and 1570 cm−1 which are attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of COO- from citrate. Moreover, in the area of low frequency, the peaks at 670, 580 and 544 cm−1 for sample A8 and the peaks at 658, 610 and 554 cm−1 for sample A9 are associated with the stretching vibration of the coordination bonds between Co–O. Fig. 6b shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of solid precipitate samples. It is observed in both solid an absorption peak at 528 nm which is associated with the presence cobalt ions complexed by citric acid [41]. Moreover, the solid A9 shows an absorption peak between 700 and 800 nm which could be related with the presence of Cu2+ ions [42]. All these results confirm the presence of metals-citrate complexes in the precipitate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cobalt solubility in citric acid solution
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of soluble cobalt in citric acid solution obtained in solubility studies at different pH values is shown in Fig. 7 . In all the samples, the initial concentration of citrate was 2 M, and the pH was adjusted using Co(OH)2. It is observed that the solubility of cobalt at pH 2.1 and 6.4 was similar, 81 and 88 mM, respectively. These values are in consonance with the cobalt concentration in equilibrium with the precipitate that appeared during the extraction experiments A8 and A9 (84 mM and pH 2.36). However, the Co solubility decreased to 0.03 M at pH 3.3. The difference in the solubility obtained could be related to the effect of the pH on the distribution of the different citrate and cobalt-citrate complex species in the medium, which could promote the formation of different cobalt-citrate salts: CoHCitrate or Co3Citrate2.

The distribution of citrate and cobalt-citrate complex species (Fig. S2) was obtained based on the equilibrium reaction listed in Table 2 to evaluate the possible formation of citrate-cobalt salts, using the pH and cobalt concentration obtained in the solubility test. Furthermore, the species distribution of citrate and cobalt-citrate complexes for CoHCitrate and Co3Citrate2 salts, previously identified in CHN analysis as possible solid species formed, was evaluated to estimate its solubility products (K
s
p ) according to the dissociation reactions (8)–(9). The reaction quotient for dissociation reaction for CoHCitrate and Co3Citrate2 salts obtained, applying the mathematical model to the experimental results together with the solubility products of CoHCitrate and Co3Citrate2, are included in Table 4 .

Comparing results from simulation studies, it can be observed that the reaction quotient for CoHCitrate for experiments carried out at pH values of 2.1 and 3.3 are similar to the value of the solubility product for CoHCitrate. This fact could indicate that the precipitate solid obtained under these conditions is CoHCitrate. Concerning the solubility studies carried out at pH value of 6.4, the quotient for Co3Citrate2 is close to the solubility product for Co3Citrate2, which could be associated with the precipitation of this specie.

To confirm these conclusions, solid samples obtained from experiments carried out at pH values of 3.3 and 6.4 were characterized by FT-IR and UV–Visible techniques. The FT-IR spectrum of both solid samples of precipitate are reported in Fig. 8 a. As can be observed, spectrums are similar, being dominated by the signal that appeared at 1570 and 1392 cm−1. As aforementioned, these signals are related to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of COO− from citrate, confirming the presence of citrates in the solids precipitate samples, but this information is not enough to distinguish if the solid are the same or not. More information about the solid precipitated was obtained by the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, presented in Fig. 8b. It can be observed a shift of the peak between 500 and 600 nm with increasing the pH, which could be caused by the formation of different cobalt-citrate complexes [41]. It could confirm the species formed in the experiments at pH 3.3 and 6.4 are not the same, which could be CoHCitrate and Co3Citrate2 salts, respectively. Finally, comparing the UV–visible spectrums of solid species formed during leaching experiments (Fig. 6b) with the UV–visible spectrum of solid precipitated at pH 3.3 and 6.4, it could be confirmed that the solid formed during leaching experiments A8 and A9 could be the same formed at pH 3.3, i.e. CoHCitrate, due to the absorption peak appeared at the same wavenumber (528 nm) in both cases. These results suggest that the precipitation of cobalt species under controlled conditions using citric acid should be explored as a promising method to recover metals contained in cathode materials of spent LIBs.

In this paper, the use of citric acid has been proposed for the extraction of lithium and cobalt from LiCoO2 particles. Different parameters such as temperature, acid concentration and the use of copper as a reductant were studied. Under the optimal conditions of 2 M citric acid, S/L ratio of 50 g/L and 70 °C, the 97.8 % and 81.3 % of Li and Co were obtained using Cu as a reductant agent. The percentage of Li and Co extracted at the same condition without adding Cu was 81.3 % and 64.5 %. These results could be explained by the effective role of copper in promoting the reduction of Co3+ to soluble Co2+. From experimental results, it has been concluded that the temperature is a key factor for leaching with citric acid due to the endothermicity of the LiCoO2 reaction. The formation of cobalt-citrate complexes during leaching experiments enhances the extraction of metals promoting the shift of citric acid equilibrium reactions and promotes the recovery of cobalt via precipitation. The formation of cobalt-citrate solid species, identified by CHN, FT-IR and UV–Vis, suggests that the use of citric acid should be further studied as a promising way to selectively recover metals from spent LIBs.

Maria del Mar Cerrillo-Gonzalez: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Methodology. Juan Manuel Paz-Garcia: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Maria Muñoz-Espinosa: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jose Miguel Rodriguez-Maroto: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Validation. Maria Villen-Guzman: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has received funding from the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under the Marie Skłodowska-Curie grant agreement No. 778045. Authors acknowledge the funding from the University of Málaga (B1-2021_35). Authors also acknowledge the support received by the SCAI-UMA (Malaga, Sapin) to perform the FTIR and UV–Vis measurements. Cerrillo-Gonzalez acknowledges the FPU2018/04295 grant obtained from the Spanish Ministry of Universities. Partial funding for open access charge: Universidad de Málaga / CBUA.
